9: David's restraint of Abishai: ʾal tašḥîtēhû kî mî šālaḥ yāḏô bimšîaḥ YHWH wᵉniqqāh — 'Do not destroy him, for who has sent his hand against YHWH's anointed and been innocent?' The title māšîaḥ YHWH (YHWH's anointed) is the covenant title that designates the king chosen and empowered by YHWH. Māšîaḥ derives from māšaḥ (to anoint with oil) — the ritual that marks covenant appointment. Saul received this title at his anointing (1 Samuel 10:1); David will receive it at his (1 Samuel 16:13). The inviolability David asserts for Saul-as-māšîaḥ applies equally to himself as the coming māšîaḥ. The word māšîaḥ in its later development becomes the formal title for the expected deliverer-king whose anointing transcends any individual (Daniel 9:25-26), ultimately pointing to Jesus who is christos (the Greek translation of māšîaḥ) par excellence.
12: The deep sleep at the Philistine camp: kî kullām yəšēnîm kî tardēmāh YHWH nāpᵉlāh ʿălêhem — 'for they were all sleeping because a deep sleep from YHWH had fallen upon them.' The noun tardēmāh is used sparingly in the OT but always for divinely-caused unconsciousness: Genesis 2:21 (the deep sleep that falls on Adam at Eve's creation), Genesis 15:12 (Abram's deep sleep at the covenant-cutting ceremony), and here. The term marks YHWH's active, protective intervention — this is not ordinary sleep but theophanic suspension. David can walk through the sleeping camp as YHWH's representative because YHWH has prepared the way. The tardēmāh connects the Michmash protection of Jonathan (1 Samuel 14:15, the theophanic panic) to the same divine-warrior sovereignty that clears the path for the anointed.
23: David's theological statement of confidence: wᵉYHWH yāšîḇ lᵉʾîš ʾet ṣiḏqātô wᵉʾet ʾĕmûnātô — 'YHWH returns to each man his righteousness (ṣeḏāqāh) and his faithfulness (ʾĕmûnāh).' The two covenant-virtue terms are significant: ṣeḏāqāh (righteousness) is right covenant conduct; ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness/steadfastness) is the covenant virtue par excellence — the same root as ʾāmēn. David is asserting that covenant faithfulness will be divinely recognized and rewarded — YHWH is the eschatological judge who sees conduct no human witness can observe (he spared Saul in the dark camp; YHWH saw). This is the OT ground for the NT principle that God rewards the hidden righteous act (Matthew 6:4 — 'your Father who sees in secret will reward you').
1: The Ziphites again report David’s location to Saul at Gibeah — hălōʾ dāwid mistattēr bᵉgibʿat haḥăḵîlāh. This is the second Ziphite betrayal (cf. 1 Samuel 23:19); the repetition shows both David’s persistent vulnerability and the ongoing threat from within Judah. The Ziphites’ willingness to betray a fellow Judahite suggests that Saul’s royal authority retained meaningful allegiance even in the south, complicating the picture of David as uniformly supported in his tribal territory.
2: Saul’s force: three thousand bᵉḥûrê yiśrāʾēl (chosen/elite men of Israel) — the same number as David’s force before the desertion in 1 Samuel 13. The precision of “three thousand” signals a serious military expedition rather than a police pursuit; Saul deploys his royal guard against one man. The description bᵉḵōl yisrāʾēl (from all Israel) emphasizes the national scope of Saul’s hunt, which David will later cite (v19) as a reason YHWH should judge between them.
3: Saul camps on the hill of Hachilah (gibʿat haḥăḵîlāh) alongside the road east of the Jeshimon (ʿal pᵉnê hayyᵉšîmōn). The Jeshimon is the desolate wasteland terrain of the eastern Judean wilderness — the barren, ravined landscape that provided both hiding and hardship. David, living in the wilderness, sees the camp when Saul arrives; his reconnaissance is immediate and shows superior tactical awareness.
4: David sends spies (mᵉragᵉlîm) and confirms that Saul has truly come (nāḵôn bāʾ šāʾûl). The use of nāḵôn (confirmed, established) is the same root used for the “established” kingdom — there is an irony that the same vocabulary of certainty applies to both the promised Davidic dynasty and the confirmed threat against David’s life. Intelligence-gathering precedes David’s every major action; he acts from confirmed information, not rumor.
5: David’s personal reconnaissance of Saul’s camp at night: he goes and sees where Saul lies, with Abner son of Ner (army commander) beside him, and the army encamped around them. The description of Saul sleeping in the camp (šōkēḇ bammַaʿgāl — sleeping in the wagon circle/encampment) with protective formation around him creates the scene David will penetrate. The precise intelligence — Abner’s position, the circular camp formation — shows David’s tactical acuity.
6: David’s question: who will go down with me to Saul’s camp? He addresses Ahimelech the Hittite (otherwise unknown) and Abishai son of Zeruiah, Joab’s brother. The question is characteristically Davidic — consultation before action, even on a volunteer basis. Abishai’s willingness (ʾănî ʾērēd ʿimmāḵ) reflects the absolute loyalty of David’s inner circle; “I will go with you” without knowing the objective is the language of covenant commitment.
7: David and Abishai penetrate the camp at night: Saul lies sleeping with his spear stuck in the ground at his head (ḥănîtô ṭᵉʾûʾāh baʾāretz mᵉraʾăšōtāyw), with Abner and the army sleeping around him. The spear at his head is the royal insignia — the same spear he hurled at David twice (1 Samuel 18:11; 1 Samuel 19:10) and at Jonathan (1 Samuel 20:33). That it stands at his head while he sleeps vulnerable before David creates a precise irony of reversal — the weapon of attempted murder is now the marker of the sleeping pursuer’s helplessness.
8: Abishai’s proposition: sāgar ʾelōhîm ʾet ʾōyiḇᵉḵā bᵉyāḏᵉḵā hayyôm — God has shut up your enemy into your hand today. The verb sāgar (to shut/enclose) is the vocabulary of divine military delivery (cf. Deuteronomy 23:15; Joshua 20:5). Abishai’s theological interpretation is not wrong in principle — God does deliver enemies — but his application is wrong in this case. He proposes pinning Saul to the ground with his own spear in a single stroke: wᵉʾiśmrennû bāḥănît wᵉʾel hāʾāreṣ ʿat pāʿam — I will not need to strike him twice.
10: David’s theological counter: ḥay YHWH kî ʾim YHWH yiggāpennû — as YHWH lives, YHWH himself will strike him down. David articulates three possible divine modes of Saul’s death: his day coming (yôm môtô), falling in battle (baMMilḥāmāh wᵉnispāh), or some other divine act. The comprehensive list — natural death, battle death, plague — covers all possibilities by which YHWH might judge Saul without David lifting a hand. The theological principle: YHWH judges kings; it is not David’s role to execute judgment on the LORD’s anointed.
11: ḥālîlāh llî mēYHWH mishᵉlaḥ yāḏî bimšîaḥ YHWH — far be it from me before YHWH that I should stretch out my hand against YHWH’s anointed. David’s restraint is explicitly grounded in the inviolability of the māšîaḥ status — not Saul’s person or power but his anointed office. The same principle that protects David (as YHWH’s future anointed) constrains him regarding Saul (as YHWH’s current anointed). Instead of the spear, David takes the spear and water jug — symbols of royal provision and military power — as evidence without executing judgment.
13: David crosses to the other side (wayyaʿăḇōr) and stands on the top of the hill far away — a great space (māqôm rāḥôq) between them, distance that ensures safety for the confrontation to follow. The topographic detail — hilltop to hilltop, separated by valley — creates the stage for the shouted dialogue. The distance also signals David’s innocence: he did not use the opportunity for assassination but removed himself and then spoke. The space between them is the space of his restraint.
14: David calls out to Abner: hălōʾ taʿăneh ʾaḇnēr — will you not answer? The rebuke is directed at the army commander responsible for guarding the king. David’s challenge from the far hilltop — “Are you a man?” — uses masculine honor vocabulary to shame Abner before his own troops. The question “who is like you in Israel?” (mî kāmōḵā bᵉyisrāʾēl) is ironic praise: Abner’s reputation makes his failure to guard the king all the more inexcusable.
15: David’s evidence: rᵉʾēh ʾet ḥănît hammelek wᵉʾet ṣappāḥat haMMayim ʾăšer mᵉraʾăšōtāyw — see the king’s spear and the water jug that was at his head. The two items he has taken are the king’s most personal possessions — the royal weapon and the drinking vessel. Their possession proves David’s complete access to Saul’s person. The rebuke of Abner’s failure — “you have not guarded your lord the king” — names the dereliction explicitly; a royal guard who fails deserves death.
16: ṭôḇ haddāḇār hazzeh ʾăšer ʿāśîtā ḥay YHWH kî ḇᵉnê māwet ʾattem — this thing you have done is not good; as the LORD lives, you sons of death (deserve death). The judgment that Abner and the guard deserve death (bᵉnê māwet — sons/deserving of death) is ironic against the background of the entire pursuit narrative: those who pursue David to bring about his death have themselves deserved death by failing in their guard duty. The moral reversal is complete — David, the target, pronounces judgment on his pursuers.
17: Saul recognizes David’s voice across the valley — hăqôlᵉḵā zeh bᵉnî dāwîḏ — is this your voice, David my son? The tender address “my son David” recurs in each of the David-Saul sparing episodes (cf. 1 Samuel 24:17), creating a tragic refrain. Saul’s recognition of the voice that could have ended his life but did not is simultaneously relief and self-indictment — the man he tried to kill has spared him again.
18: David’s legal brief: lāmmāh zeh ʾăḏōnî rōḏēp ʾaḥărê ʿaḇdô — why does my lord pursue his servant? The questions that follow — what have I done? what harm is in my hands? — are the standard formulas of an innocent party appealing for legal vindication (cf. Psalms 7:3-5). David frames the pursuit as legally unjustified, inviting Saul to articulate the charge or abandon the prosecution.
19: David’s theological argument about the meaning of exile: if YHWH has stirred Saul against David, let an offering appease him (yāriaḥ minḥāh). But if humans (bᵉnê hāʾāḏām) have incited him, they are cursed before YHWH — for they have driven David out (gēraš) from attaching himself to the inheritance of YHWH (lᵉhisṯappēaḥ bᵉnaḥălat YHWH), saying “Go, serve other gods.” This is David’s deepest theological complaint: exile from the land is exile from covenantal worship of YHWH, effectively a forced apostasy. The argument treats territorial displacement as a theological crisis, not merely a personal hardship.
20: wᵉʿattāh ʾal yippōl dāmî ʾarṣāh minneged pᵉnê YHWH — now do not let my blood fall to the ground away from the presence of YHWH. The phrase minneged pᵉnê YHWH (away from the presence of YHWH) identifies death in exile as the worst fate — dying outside the land is dying “far from YHWH’s face.” The image of the king of Israel hunting a single flea (parʿōš) or a partridge (qōrēʾ) in the mountains is deliberately bathetic — the mighty army pursuing a tiny bird captures the disproportionality of Saul’s vendetta.
21: Saul’s third confession: ḥāṭāʾtî šûḇ bᵉnî dāwid — I have sinned; come back, my son David. The third use of the confession formula in the David-Saul narrative (cf. 1 Samuel 15:24-30; 1 Samuel 24:17) marks a pattern of recurring contrition without repentance. Each confession is followed by resumption of pursuit. Lōʾ ʾāraʿ lᵉḵā ʿôḏ — I will not do you harm again — is the promise that the narrative has already shown to be unreliable. Saul’s self-assessment — “I have played the fool (sāḵaltî) and erred greatly (wāʾešgeh harbēh mᵉʾōḏ)” — uses the vocabulary of wisdom failure alongside moral failure.
22: David’s response holds both mercy and distance: here is the king’s spear (hinnēh ḥănît hammelek) — let one of the young men come over and get it. David returns the royal property but does not return himself. The return of the spear is an act of symbolic reconciliation — the weapon that Saul threw at David twice is returned as an act of mercy — but David refuses the personal proximity that would make Saul’s promise meaningful. Wisdom about unreliable repentance is not lack of forgiveness; David forgives without trusting.
24: wᵉhinnēh kaʾăšer gāḏᵉlāh napšᵉḵā hayyôm hayyōm bᵉʿênāy kēn tigdal napšî bᵉʿênê YHWH — as your life was precious in my eyes today, so may my life be precious in the eyes of YHWH. The mutual-value formula creates a covenant of reciprocity: as I valued you, may YHWH value me. The appeal to YHWH’s valuation of David’s life is a prayer grounded in precedent — David’s mercy to Saul becomes the basis of his appeal for YHWH’s mercy to him. This is the theology of the Psalms’ lament tradition in narrative form.
25: Saul’s final blessing of David in this episode: bārûḵ ʾattāh bᵉnî dāwid gam ʿāśōh taʿăśeh wᵉgam yāḵōl tûḵāl — blessed are you, my son David; you will indeed accomplish and you will indeed prevail. The cognate infinitives (ʿāśōh taʿăśeh — do doing; yāḵōl tûḵāl — prevail prevailing) express emphatic certainty. Saul’s blessing of the man he has been trying to kill is the ironic climax of the episode — the rejected king pronouncing the inevitable triumph of his replacement. David returns to his way; Saul to his place. The separation is final.
